resume:
Alexandra Whitman
VP of Data & Analytics
Seattle, WA

LEADERSHIP SUMMARY
Data and analytics executive with seventeen years spanning analytics, data engineering, data science, and governance across high-growth software and marketplace businesses. I have spent the last seven years as a senior data leader, currently as VP of Data & Analytics, where I own the strategy, organization, and platform that turn data into decisions across the company. My focus as a leader is on building durable data foundations, raising the analytical bar of the organizations I partner with, and connecting data investment to business outcomes. I operate as a partner to product, finance, and go-to-market leadership, and I am comfortable in the boardroom translating a data roadmap into business value.

HOW I LEAD
I believe the job of a data leader is to create leverage. That means investing in the right platform so analysts aren't reinventing pipelines, hiring and growing strong leaders rather than doing the work myself, and relentlessly tying our roadmap to the company's priorities. I care a great deal about culture — I want a team where people feel ownership and where data is trusted rather than argued about. I try to lead with clarity of vision and to bring the organization along so that data becomes part of how every team thinks, not a service desk they file tickets with.

EXPERIENCE
Harborline (B2C marketplace) — VP of Data & Analytics (2020-present)
- Own the company's data strategy and lead a multidisciplinary organization spanning analytics, data engineering, data science, and data governance.
- Set the vision for the data platform and partner with engineering leadership on its evolution.
- Sit on the senior leadership team and represent data in company planning and board updates.
- Lead the analytics partnership with product, growth, finance, and operations.
- Drove a multi-year program to modernize the data platform and elevate self-serve analytics across the company.
- Established the company's data governance and data-quality function.

Nimbus Software (B2B SaaS) — Senior Director, Analytics & Data Engineering (2016-2020)
- Built and led the central analytics and data-engineering organization supporting product and revenue teams.
- Stood up the company's experimentation and metrics practice.
- Partnered with executives on the metrics that defined company performance.

Vantage Commerce — Director, Business Intelligence (2012-2016)
- Led the BI and reporting function and the migration to a cloud data warehouse.
- Built the reporting layer used across the executive team.

Earlier roles — Analytics Manager and Data Analyst across two retail and media companies, where I grew from individual contributor analyst into people leadership.

ORGANIZATIONS I HAVE BUILT
Across these roles I have organized data teams in a few different ways depending on the stage of the company. Early on I ran a centralized model where a single analytics team served every function, which is efficient but creates a queue and distance from the business. As companies matured I moved toward an embedded or hub-and-spoke model, where analysts sit close to product, growth, and finance while a central platform-and-governance core keeps standards, tooling, and definitions consistent. I have learned that the org design is itself a leadership decision with real tradeoffs: centralization buys consistency and career depth for analysts; embedding buys speed and context but risks fragmenting metric definitions. Choosing between them, and knowing when to re-org from one to the other, is a large part of what a data leader actually does.

HOW I PARTNER WITH THE BUSINESS
I spend a meaningful share of my time outside the data org — in product reviews, in finance planning cycles, and in go-to-market operating meetings — because I believe data only changes decisions when it shows up early in the conversation rather than as a report after the decision is made. I try to make the data team a thinking partner to those functions, not a ticket queue, and I push my leaders to understand the business questions deeply enough that they can challenge a request rather than just fulfill it.

WHAT I FOCUS ON AS A LEADER
Strategy and prioritization: making sure the data organization is working on what matters most to the business rather than the loudest request. Platform and leverage: investing so that the marginal analysis gets cheaper over time. Talent: hiring, growing, and retaining strong data leaders and practitioners. Trust and governance: making the numbers something the company believes in. Business partnership: being close enough to product and finance that data shapes decisions early, not after the fact.

SELECTED THEMES
- Data platform modernization and the move toward self-serve analytics.
- Building an experimentation and metrics culture.
- Establishing data governance and data quality as a function.
- Connecting the data roadmap to company strategy and outcomes.
- Developing the next layer of data leadership.

SKILLS & DOMAINS
Data strategy and organizational design; analytics and experimentation; data engineering and platform; data science and ML enablement; data governance and quality; executive communication and stakeholder management; cloud data warehousing and the modern data stack.

EDUCATION
M.S., Statistics.
B.A., Economics.

BOARDS & SPEAKING
Frequent speaker on building data organizations and the modern data stack; advisor to early-stage data tooling companies.

WHAT I'M LOOKING FOR
A VP or Chief Data Officer role where I can own data end-to-end for a company that genuinely wants to be data-driven, partner with a strong executive team, and build an organization known for both rigor and impact.
jd: Cobalt (B2C fintech) is hiring a VP of Data & Analytics to own data strategy, platform, and the analytics/data-science/governance organization (~50 people) reporting to the CTO. You will be accountable for measurable outcomes, not just a roadmap: how data investment changed decision speed, how self-serve adoption moved, how experimentation lifted product metrics, and how you sized and verified the business value of platform work. In this interview we will push hard for specifics — a particular initiative you led, the exact decision you made, who did the work, the metric that moved, and the timeframe. We are wary of executives who speak only in vision and vocabulary. Requirements: 12+ years in data leadership, experience modernizing a data platform, and a demonstrated ability to connect data work to verifiable business impact.

history.q: You led a multi-year data platform modernization at Harborline. What was the single most important business problem it was meant to solve?
Pick one concrete initiative inside that program and tell me what you actually decided and did.
What metric did that move, and by how much over what period?
Who on your team led the execution, and what specifically was your decision versus theirs?
Let's get specific: name one data investment you made, the dollar or headcount it cost, and the measurable return. Walk me through it.

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, so at the VP level it's really about creating leverage and tying the data roadmap to the business. The way I think about it, you invest in the right foundations, you bring the org along, and you let the outcomes follow. We made some big bets on the platform and on self-serve, and the impact was significant — adoption went up, trust in the numbers went up, and the business felt the difference. I'm less of a in-the-weeds person at this level; my job is to set the vision and empower strong leaders to execute. So rather than a single number, I'd frame it as: we fundamentally changed how the company uses data, and that's the kind of durable impact I optimize for.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: rather than a single number, I'd frame it as

expected-yield: one concrete data investment with its cost (dollars or headcount) and a single verified return metric — e.g. self-serve adoption percentage before vs. after, or decision/query latency reduction — with the timeframe, forcing the candidate off vision-level abstraction into a measurable result
